question:
Problem B-5
Suppose that \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n} \) are real \( (n>1) \) and
\[
A+\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}^{2}<\frac{1}{n-1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}\right)^{2}
\]

Prove that \( A<2 a_{i} a_{j} \) for \( 1<i<j<n \).
solution:
B-5.
From the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality, one has
\[
\left[\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)+a_{3}+a_{4}+\cdots+a_{n}\right]^{2} \leqslant\left[1^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+1^{2}\right]\left[\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+\cdots+a_{n}^{2}\right]
\]
or
\[
\left(\sum a_{i}\right)^{2} \leqslant(n-1)\left[\left(\sum a_{i}^{2}\right)+2 a_{1} a_{2}\right] \quad \text { or } \quad[1 /(n-1)]\left(\sum a_{i}\right)^{2} \leqslant\left(\sum a_{i}^{2}\right)+2 a_{1} a_{2}
\]

Using the hypothesis, one then has
\[
A<-\left(\sum a_{i}^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{n-1}\left(\sum a_{i}\right)^{2} \leqslant-\left(\sum a_{i}^{2}\right)+\left(\sum a_{i}^{2}\right)+2 a_{1} a_{2}=2 a_{1} a_{2} .
\]

Similarly, \( A<2 a_{i} a_{j} \) for \( 1 \leqslant i<j \leqslant n \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-5
Suppose that \( firstcoef, secondcoef, \ldots, lastcoef \) are real \( (arraysize>1) \) and
\[
fixedvalue+\sum_{indexvar=1}^{arraysize} variablecoef^{2}<\frac{1}{arraysize-1}\left(\sum_{indexvar=1}^{arraysize} variablecoef\right)^{2}
\]

Prove that \( fixedvalue<2\,variablecoef\,anothercoef \) for \( 1<indexvar<secondidx<arraysize \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-5.
From the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality, one has
\[
\left[\left(firstcoef+secondcoef\right)+a_{3}+a_{4}+\cdots+lastcoef\right]^{2}\leqslant\left[1^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+1^{2}\right]\left[\left(firstcoef+secondcoef\right)^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+\cdots+lastcoef^{2}\right]
\]
or
\[
\left(\sum variablecoef\right)^{2}\leqslant(arraysize-1)\left[\left(\sum variablecoef^{2}\right)+2\,firstcoef\,secondcoef\right]\quad\text{or}\quad[1 /(arraysize-1)]\left(\sum variablecoef\right)^{2}\leqslant\left(\sum variablecoef^{2}\right)+2\,firstcoef\,secondcoef
\]

Using the hypothesis, one then has
\[
fixedvalue< -\left(\sum variablecoef^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{arraysize-1}\left(\sum variablecoef\right)^{2}\leqslant -\left(\sum variablecoef^{2}\right)+\left(\sum variablecoef^{2}\right)+2\,firstcoef\,secondcoef=2\,firstcoef\,secondcoef .
\]

Similarly, \( fixedvalue<2\,variablecoef\,anothercoef \) for \( 1\leqslant indexvar<secondidx\leqslant arraysize \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-5
Suppose that \( windmill, lighthouse, \ldots, sailboat \) are real \( (harborage>1) \) and
\[
pendulum+\sum_{sunflower=1}^{harborage} fernleaf^{2}<\frac{1}{harborage-1}\left(\sum_{sunflower=1}^{harborage} fernleaf\right)^{2}
\]

Prove that \( pendulum<2 fernleaf raincloud \) for \( 1<sunflower<stargazer<harborage \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-5.
From the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality, one has
\[
\left[\left(windmill+lighthouse\right)+a_{3}+a_{4}+\cdots+sailboat\right]^{2} \leqslant\left[1^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+1^{2}\right]\left[\left(windmill+lighthouse\right)^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+\cdots+sailboat^{2}\right]
\]
or
\[
\left(\sum fernleaf\right)^{2} \leqslant(harborage-1)\left[\left(\sum fernleaf^{2}\right)+2 windmill lighthouse\right] \quad \text { or } \quad[1 /(harborage-1)]\left(\sum fernleaf\right)^{2} \leqslant\left(\sum fernleaf^{2}\right)+2 windmill lighthouse
\]

Using the hypothesis, one then has
\[
pendulum<-\left(\sum fernleaf^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{harborage-1}\left(\sum fernleaf\right)^{2} \leqslant-\left(\sum fernleaf^{2}\right)+\left(\sum fernleaf^{2}\right)+2 windmill lighthouse=2 windmill lighthouse .
\]

Similarly, \( pendulum<2 fernleaf raincloud \) for \( 1 \leqslant sunflower<stargazer \leqslant harborage \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-5
Suppose that \( terminalvalue, initialvalue, \ldots, beginningvalue \) are real \( (boundlessnumber>1) \) and
\[
mutableamount+\sum_{wholeindex=1}^{boundlessnumber} fixedvalue^{2}<\frac{1}{boundlessnumber-1}\left(\sum_{wholeindex=1}^{boundlessnumber} fixedvalue\right)^{2}
\]

Prove that \( mutableamount<2 fixedvalue settledvalue \) for \( 1<wholeindex<completeindex<boundlessnumber \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-5.
From the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality, one has
\[
\left[\left(terminalvalue+initialvalue\right)+a_{3}+a_{4}+\cdots+beginningvalue\right]^{2} \leqslant\left[1^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+1^{2}\right]\left[\left(terminalvalue+initialvalue\right)^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+\cdots+beginningvalue^{2}\right]
\]
or
\[
\left(\sum fixedvalue\right)^{2} \leqslant(boundlessnumber-1)\left[\left(\sum fixedvalue^{2}\right)+2 terminalvalue initialvalue\right] \quad \text { or } \quad[1 /(boundlessnumber-1)]\left(\sum fixedvalue\right)^{2} \leqslant\left(\sum fixedvalue^{2}\right)+2 terminalvalue initialvalue
\]

Using the hypothesis, one then has
\[
mutableamount<-\left(\sum fixedvalue^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{boundlessnumber-1}\left(\sum fixedvalue\right)^{2} \leqslant-\left(\sum fixedvalue^{2}\right)+\left(\sum fixedvalue^{2}\right)+2 terminalvalue initialvalue=2 terminalvalue initialvalue .
\]

Similarly, \( mutableamount<2 fixedvalue settledvalue \) for \( 1 \leqslant wholeindex<completeindex \leqslant boundlessnumber \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-5
Suppose that \( fghqweop, mnbvrtyu, \ldots, xcvsdfrt \) are real \( (qzxctbhu>1) \) and
\[
plkjsdwe+\sum_{sduifghk=1}^{qzxctbhu} ljhqwept^{2}<\frac{1}{qzxctbhu-1}\left(\sum_{sduifghk=1}^{qzxctbhu} ljhqwept\right)^{2}
\]

Prove that \( plkjsdwe<2 ljhqwept zmxnertl \) for \( 1<sduifghk<gperyand<qzxctbhu \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-5.
From the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality, one has
\[
\left[\left(fghqweop+mnbvrtyu\right)+a_{3}+a_{4}+\cdots+xcvsdfrt\right]^{2} \leqslant\left[1^{2}+1^{2}+\cdots+1^{2}\right]\left[\left(fghqweop+mnbvrtyu\right)^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+\cdots+xcvsdfrt^{2}\right]
\]
or
\[
\left(\sum ljhqwept\right)^{2} \leqslant(qzxctbhu-1)\left[\left(\sum ljhqwept^{2}\right)+2 fghqweop mnbvrtyu\right] \quad \text { or } \quad[1 /(qzxctbhu-1)]\left(\sum ljhqwept\right)^{2} \leqslant\left(\sum ljhqwept^{2}\right)+2 fghqweop mnbvrtyu
\]

Using the hypothesis, one then has
\[
plkjsdwe<-\left(\sum ljhqwept^{2}\right)+\frac{1}{qzxctbhu-1}\left(\sum ljhqwept\right)^{2} \leqslant-\left(\sum ljhqwept^{2}\right)+\left(\sum ljhqwept^{2}\right)+2 fghqweop mnbvrtyu=2 fghqweop mnbvrtyu .
\]

Similarly, \( plkjsdwe<2 ljhqwept zmxnertl \) for \( 1 \leqslant sduifghk<gperyand \leqslant qzxctbhu \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let n be an integer greater than 1 and let x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n}\in\mathbb R satisfy

B+\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{i}^{2}<\frac{1}{n-1}\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_{i}\Bigr)^{2}.

Prove that

B<2x_{i}x_{j}\qquad\text{for every }1\le i<j\le n.
kernel_variant.solution:
We treat the cases n=2 and n\ge 3 separately.

Case 1: n = 2.
The hypothesis reads
B+x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}<\frac{1}{1}(x_{1}+x_{2})^{2}=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+2x_{1}x_{2}.
Subtract x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2} from both sides to get B<2x_{1}x_{2}, which is exactly the desired conclusion.

Case 2: n \ge 3.
Fix two distinct indices i,j with 1\le i<j\le n.  To avoid clutter we illustrate the argument with (i,j)=(2,3); the same computation works for every pair by symmetry.

Write the total sum of the x_{k} as
(x_{2}+x_{3})+x_{1}+x_{4}+\dots +x_{n}.

Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality to the vectors
(1,1,\dots ,1) (consisting of n-1 ones) and
((x_{2}+x_{3}),\,x_{1},\,x_{4},\dots ,x_{n}):

[(x_{2}+x_{3})+x_{1}+x_{4}+\dots +x_{n}]^{2}\le (n-1)[(x_{2}+x_{3})^{2}+x_{1}^{2}+x_{4}^{2}+\dots +x_{n}^{2}].

Hence
\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}\Bigr)^{2}\le (n-1)\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}^{2}+2x_{2}x_{3}\Bigr),
so that
\frac{1}{n-1}\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}\Bigr)^{2}\le \sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}^{2}+2x_{2}x_{3}.\tag{1}

Starting from the hypothesis and subtracting \sum x_{k}^{2} we obtain
B< -\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}^{2}+\frac{1}{n-1}\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}\Bigr)^{2}.

Insert the upper bound (1) for the last term:
B< -\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}^{2}+\Bigl(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{k}^{2}+2x_{2}x_{3}\Bigr)=2x_{2}x_{3}.

Because the whole argument is symmetric in the indices, replacing (2,3) with any pair (i,j) shows
B<2x_{i}x_{j}\qquad(1\le i<j\le n).

Combining the two cases completes the proof for all n>1.

core_steps: Group any two variables and use Cauchy–Schwarz on (1,1,…,1) vs. (a_p+a_q , remaining a_k)
Derive (Σ a_i)^2 / (n−1) ≤ Σ a_i^2 + 2 a_p a_q
Combine with the hypothesis A + Σ a_i^2 < (Σ a_i)^2 / (n−1) to get A < 2 a_p a_q
Apply symmetry (rename indices) to cover all pairs 1 ≤ i<j ≤ n

slot1.description: Which two indices are paired in the Cauchy–Schwarz step

slot2.description: Label chosen for the constant being estimated
